title: Anxiety in Women: the Under-Discussed Shape
subtitle: On the specific ways anxiety presents in women, and why it goes unrecognised
excerpt: A reading on the presentation of anxiety in women, particularly the hypervigilance-as-normal pattern and the physical symptoms that don't get connected to anxiety. On why women's anxiety is under-diagnosed, undertreated, and often misread as personality traits.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education, drawing on research on gendered presentations of anxiety including Tracy Dennis-Tiwary's Future Tense: Why Anxiety is Good for You (Even Though It Feels Bad) (2022) and the gender gap in anxiety diagnosis literature.

practice.whenToUse: Read when anxiety is present and you want to understand what you're dealing with — particularly if you've been anxious for a long time without naming it as such.

content.text: Anxiety is the most common mental health condition globally and is significantly more prevalent in women than men. Despite this, women's anxiety often goes unrecognised: by healthcare providers, by the women themselves, and by the people around them. This is partly because the way anxiety presents in women is often not the way anxiety is culturally understood.

The cultural image of anxiety is acute: panic attacks, phobias, visible distress. This presentation exists, but it is not the dominant one. More common in women is a chronic, low-level version: persistent worry that moves from one concern to another, difficulty settling or resting, sensitivity to perceived conflict or criticism, a body that stays mildly alert even in safe situations, and the constant mental tracking of what needs doing, what might go wrong, and what others need.

Many women carrying this form of anxiety have never named it as such. It has been normalised as personality: described as being a worrier, a planner, a high-achiever, or someone who cares a lot. These descriptions are not wrong, but they miss the layer of physiological activation underneath them.

Several factors compound the under-recognition. Women are socialised to manage and conceal distress rather than express it in ways that would prompt help-seeking. The hypervigilance and people-monitoring that are characteristic of anxious processing are often rewarded in women, producing behaviour that gets described as caring, responsible, and socially attuned. The anxiety running underneath the praised behaviour goes unremarked.

Anxiety also has significant physical symptoms (chest tightness, digestive upset, muscle tension, fatigue, disturbed sleep) that are often investigated medically and dismissed when no physical cause is found, without the anxiety being named as the likely source.

Anxiety is a nervous system state. The amygdala, the brain's threat-detection system, has registered something as requiring alertness, and the body has responded accordingly. This can happen without a consciously identifiable threat. It can happen as a low background hum rather than an acute spike. And it can become so habitual that the body maintains a mild alert state as its default.

Understanding anxiety as a physiological state rather than a character trait changes the approach to working with it. Character is hard to change. Nervous system states are workable: through breath, through somatic practices, through the gradual accumulation of experiences of safety.

Naming anxiety accurately, as a nervous system pattern rather than a personality defect, tends to reduce the shame attached to it. The secondary shame about being anxious is often more disabling than the anxiety itself. Women who identify their chronic worry as anxiety rather than weakness typically report greater access to both self-compassion and practical support.

Written for homemade.education, drawing on research on gender and anxiety prevalence, Tracy Dennis-Tiwary's Future Tense (2022), and the somatic and nervous-system-regulation tradition in anxiety treatment.
